text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 66-year-old female with recently resected pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head/uncinate process (pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB). Status post pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 10 weeks prior, complicated by grade B pancreatic fistula now resolved. History of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) followed by concurrent cape-based chemoradiation (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions, IMRT). Baseline post-surgical surveillance imaging prior to initiation of adjuvant chemotherapy. CA 19-9 and CEA obtained at time of imaging.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed following the administration of intravenous iodinated contrast material (Omnipaque 350, 125 mL) in the portal venous phase. Oral contrast was not administered. Axial images were obtained with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations. Slice thickness 3 mm axial, 3 mm coronal and sagittal reformats. Comparison: Prior CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol obtained approximately 6 weeks post-chemoradiation (prior to surgical resection), and prior pancreatic protocol CT obtained after completion of 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX.

FINDINGS:

Post-Surgical Anatomy and Operative Bed:
The patient is status post pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy. Expected postoperative anatomical reconfiguration is present. The hepaticojejunostomy is identified in the right upper quadrant without biliary ductal dilatation; the common hepatic duct measures approximately 4 mm and is within normal limits for a post-anastomotic caliber. The pancreaticojejunostomy is identified in the central abdomen and appears intact without surrounding fluid collection or abscess. The gastrojejunostomy is identified in the left upper quadrant, also appearing patent and unremarkable. The gastric remnant is decompressed without wall thickening or luminal abnormality. There is expected postsurgical fat stranding and soft tissue remodeling throughout the right upper quadrant and periduodenal/peripancreatic bed, representing mature postoperative fibrosis. No focal fluid collection, hematoma, seroma, or abscess is identified in the surgical bed or along the mesenteric root. The previously described grade B pancreatic fistula — for which a surgical drain was maintained postoperatively — has resolved; the drain tract has closed and there is no residual peripancreatic collection. The small mesenteric vessels in the operative field demonstrate expected postsurgical remodeling. The superior mesenteric artery and superior mesenteric vein remain patent and widely opacified without hemodynamically significant stenosis or occlusion. The portal vein is patent and normally opacified with no intraluminal filling defect to suggest portal vein thrombosis.

Pancreatic Remnant:
The body and tail of the pancreas remain intact and are visualized in the expected location. The remnant pancreatic parenchyma appears mildly atrophic, which is consistent with prior surgery and the patient's known post-pancreatectomy exocrine insufficiency (on pancrelipase supplementation). The pancreatic duct within the remnant is not dilated. No focal parenchymal lesion or suspicious enhancement abnormality is identified within the remnant pancreas. There is mild peripancreatic soft tissue stranding along the anastomotic region at the pancreaticojejunostomy, which is stable in character compared to the prior postoperative imaging and consistent with residual postsurgical change without evidence of anastomotic leak or recurrent fistula.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and contour. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously in the portal venous phase. No focal hypoattenuating, hyperattenuating, or rim-enhancing lesions are identified within the liver. No wedge-shaped perfusion defects or capsular retraction to suggest metastatic involvement. The hepatic veins and intrahepatic portal branches appear patent. The previously treated bile duct (anastomosed at hepaticojejunostomy) demonstrates no proximal intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. In particular, the right and left hepatic ducts are not dilated.

Gallbladder:
The gallbladder is surgically absent (status post cholecystectomy performed at time of Whipple procedure). No residual gallbladder fossa collection or biloma.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size and attenuation, measuring approximately 10.2 cm in long axis. No focal splenic lesion identified.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable in size and morphology. No focal nodule or mass.

Kidneys and Ureters:
The kidneys are symmetric in size and enhance symmetrically without focal cortical abnormality. No hydronephrosis. No renal or ureteral calculi. No perirenal collection.

Bladder and Pelvic Organs:
The urinary bladder is partially distended and smooth-walled. No intraluminal mass. The uterus and adnexa are not well-visualized given patient age and absence of dedicated pelvic imaging, but no gross pelvic mass is apparent. No free pelvic fluid.

Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the retroperitoneal, mesenteric, iliac, inguinal, or porta hepatis stations. No discrete lymph node mass suspicious for metastatic involvement. The small mesenteric and retroperitoneal lymph nodes in the operative field measure up to 6 mm in short axis, within normal limits and favored to reflect reactive or postoperative change. No celiac, para-aortic, or portocaval nodal enlargement.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
No peritoneal nodularity, omental caking, or ascites. The mesentery is unremarkable without soft tissue infiltration or nodular deposits. No serosal implants identified along the bowel surfaces, diaphragm, or pelvic peritoneum.

Vascular Structures:
The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal change. Moderate atherosclerotic calcification is noted along the infrarenal aorta and iliac vessels. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery all appear patent. The portal vein and SMV are patent as noted above. No venous thrombosis identified.

Bowel:
Small bowel loops in the right abdomen demonstrate expected post-Whipple configuration. Postsurgical adhesions cannot be excluded clinically but no high-grade obstruction or bowel dilation is apparent. The colon appears unremarkable throughout its course without wall thickening, mass, or dilatation.

Chest Bases (Included on This Study):
The imaged lung bases are clear. No pleural effusion. No consolidation or pulmonary nodule identified at the lung bases on this non-dedicated chest acquisition.

Bony Structures:
Degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine and sacroiliac joints, appropriate for patient age. No osteolytic or osteoblastic lesion suspicious for osseous metastatic involvement.

IMPRESSION:

1. Expected post-pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) anatomical changes with an intact hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy, and gastrojejunostomy. No anastomotic leak, peripancreatic fluid collection, or abscess. The previously documented grade B pancreatic fistula has fully resolved with no residual collection.

2. No hepatic lesion identified. The liver parenchyma appears homogeneous without focal masses or perfusion abnormalities to suggest hepatic metastatic disease.

3. No peritoneal implants, omental deposits, or ascites. No radiographic evidence of locoregional recurrence in the operative bed or mesentery.

4. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the retroperitoneal, mesenteric, celiac, or porta hepatis regions.

5. Patent superior mesenteric artery, superior mesenteric vein, and portal vein without thrombosis or hemodynamically significant stenosis.

6. Mild remnant pancreatic parenchymal atrophy, consistent with post-pancreatectomy changes.

7. Overall, this study demonstrates expected post-surgical changes following Whipple procedure without imaging evidence of residual or recurrent malignancy. Correlate with serum tumor markers (CA 19-9, CEA) as clinically indicated.

Serum tumor markers obtained at time of this examination:
  - CA 19-9: 28 U/mL (reference range: <37 U/mL) — normalized
  - CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (reference range: <3.0 ng/mL) — within normal limits

Correlation with these normalized tumor markers further supports the absence of detectable residual or recurrent disease at this time.
